sourceLabel: USCG/NMC Q445 Lifeboat Operator Sample Exam
relationshipToCourse: Official Lifeboat Operator sample exam material; useful for exam alignment but not Vinci's exact manual
sourceNotes: Official-source intake used to calibrate exam-style difficulty, terminology, and procedures.
sourceCitation: United States Coast Guard, National Maritime Center, Q445 Lifeboat Operator (Sample Examination), dated 8/26/2025. Per the document's own footer: adapted for testing purposes only from HAYLER, American Merchant Seaman's Manual, 7th edition, Copyright (c) 2003 by Cornell Maritime Press, Inc. Further reproduction prohibited without permission — this intake file quotes/paraphrases exam items for internal study-tool development only.
sourceScope: 50 multiple-choice items plus one referenced illustration (D012SA, a gravity-davit/release-gear diagram). Three items (3, 13, 17) are explicitly tagged 'BOTH INTERNATIONAL & INLAND' in the source, meaning they are Rules-of-the-Road / navigation-rules content rather than water-survival-specific content — flagged individually below since they fall outside the Water Survival Student Manual's given table of contents.

items.originalQuestion: Your ship is sinking rapidly. A container containing an inflatable liferaft has bobbed to the surface upon functioning of the hydrostatic release. Which action should you take?
items.originalAnswer: Take no action because the painter will cause the liferaft to inflate and open the container.
items.cleanedQuestion: Your ship is sinking rapidly and a liferaft container has bobbed to the surface after the hydrostatic release functioned. What should you do?
items.cleanedAnswer: Take no action — the painter line will cause the liferaft to inflate and open the container on its own.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Strong misconception-trap: cutting the painter (a tempting-sounding 'safety' action) is exactly wrong, since the painter is what triggers inflation.

items.originalQuestion: You are involved in an emergency landing of a helicopter on the water. When should you inflate your life jacket?
items.originalAnswer: After exiting clear of the helicopter
items.cleanedQuestion: During an emergency helicopter water landing (ditching), when should you inflate your life jacket?
items.cleanedAnswer: After exiting clear of the helicopter — not before or during exit.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Chapter mapping is imperfect — this is about ditching/egress from a sinking helicopter, not a hoist rescue of a survivor already in the water. No better-fitting subtopic exists in the given manual TOC excerpt. Real trap value: an inflated life jacket can trap someone inside a sinking/rolling aircraft.

items.originalQuestion: BOTH INTERNATIONAL & INLAND Which is TRUE when a vessel uses any sound or light signals to attract the attention of another vessel?
items.originalAnswer: The signal cannot be mistaken for a signal authorized by the Rules
items.cleanedQuestion: Under the Navigation Rules (International & Inland), what must be true of any sound or light signal used to attract another vessel's attention?
items.cleanedAnswer: It cannot be mistaken for a signal already authorized elsewhere in the Rules.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: OUT-OF-SCOPE FLAG: explicitly tagged 'BOTH INTERNATIONAL & INLAND' in the source, meaning this is Navigation Rules (COLREGS/Inland Rules) content, not water-survival content per the manual's given table of contents. Q445 appears to draw from a broader Lifeboatman/seamanship question pool than just the Water Survival manual. Recommend holding this out unless Vinci's course explicitly covers Rules of the Road.

items.originalQuestion: Which represents the appropriate time for setting off distress flares and rockets?
items.originalAnswer: Only when there is a chance of their being seen by rescue vessels
items.cleanedQuestion: When is the appropriate time to set off distress flares and rockets?
items.cleanedAnswer: Only when there is a real chance they will be seen by rescue vessels or aircraft — not on a fixed schedule.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Directly confirms the 'conserve limited flares for real opportunities' logic already used in the live app's signaling content (independently authored, not SIU-derived) — official-source support for that reasoning pattern, even without a literal source match.

items.originalQuestion: Which of the following is part of the annual SOLAS required test on an EPIRB?
items.originalAnswer: Verification that the unit has been maintained by a shore based maintenance provider
items.cleanedQuestion: What is part of the annual SOLAS-required test on an EPIRB?
items.cleanedAnswer: Verification that the unit has been maintained by a shore-based maintenance provider.
items.mappedSubtopic: Emergency Position Indicating Radio Beacon / EPIRB
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Related equipment (EPIRB) to SIU item 75, but a different fact (annual maintenance requirement vs. activating it before abandoning ship) — not a direct overlap.

items.originalQuestion: What statement about immersion suits is TRUE?
items.originalAnswer: No stowage container for immersion suits may be capable of being locked.
items.cleanedQuestion: Which statement about immersion suits is TRUE?
items.cleanedAnswer: No stowage container for immersion suits may be capable of being locked — access must never be blocked in an emergency.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Good trap: the wrong answers (routine deck wear, any tear requiring replacement, oiling the zipper) all sound plausible but are false per this source.

items.originalQuestion: When is it recommended that the external flotation bladder on an immersion suit be inflated?
items.originalAnswer: Upon entry into the water
items.cleanedQuestion: When should the external flotation bladder on an immersion suit be inflated?
items.cleanedAnswer: Upon entry into the water, not beforehand.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: None significant.

items.originalQuestion: When using the lifeboat compass, you must be careful to __________.
items.originalAnswer: All of the above (set it on the centerline of the boat / keep metal objects away from it / apply the correction for compass error)
items.cleanedQuestion: When using the lifeboat compass, what must you be careful to do?
items.cleanedAnswer: Set it on the centerline of the boat, keep metal objects away from it, AND apply the correction for compass error — all three.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: NEAR-VERBATIM MATCH to SIU intake item 24 ('Set it on the centerline of the boat, apply the correction for compass error, keep metal objects away from it'). This is a genuine multi-source agreement: an official USCG sample exam independently states the same three-part answer as the unverified SIU practice test. Strong candidate to promote SIU-24-derived content to multi-source-supported.

items.originalQuestion: How is the inside light in an inflatable liferaft activated?
items.originalAnswer: Automatically as the liferaft inflates
items.cleanedQuestion: How is the inside light in an inflatable liferaft activated?
items.cleanedAnswer: Automatically, as the liferaft inflates.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: None significant.

items.originalQuestion: Upon completion of fueling a gasoline driven vessel it is necessary to perform which task?
items.originalAnswer: Ventilate before starting engine
items.cleanedQuestion: After finishing fueling a gasoline-driven vessel, what must be done before starting the engine?
items.cleanedAnswer: Ventilate the vessel before starting the engine.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: This is about a gasoline-driven vessel generally, not specifically a diesel lifeboat engine (lifeboats in this source and SIU are diesel — see items 28/40 and SIU 6/44) — likely broader small-craft/rescue-boat safety content. Companion to item 40 (post-fueling ventilation of compartments); the two are sequential steps of the same safety procedure, not conflicting.

items.originalQuestion: What is TRUE concerning frapping lines?
items.originalAnswer: They are used to steady a lifeboat when lowered.
items.cleanedQuestion: What is the function of frapping lines?
items.cleanedAnswer: They are used to steady a lifeboat while it is being lowered.
items.mappedSubtopic: Launching a Lifeboat from a Gravity Davit
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Complements SIU item 16 (WHERE frapping lines are rigged: at the embarkation deck) with WHAT they do (steady the boat). Also directly relevant to the live app's q-gloss-frapping question (already promoted from the SIU intake) — this official source gives the function that question's explanation should include.

items.originalQuestion: In illustration D012SA below, what is the mechanism that will release the tricing pendant?
items.originalAnswer: The McCluny hook
items.cleanedQuestion: On a gravity-davit lifeboat rig, what mechanism releases the tricing pendant?
items.cleanedAnswer: The McCluny hook.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Original question references illustration D012SA (a labeled diagram of davit/release hardware, numbered parts 1-9, no text legend identifying each number). The question and answer choices are self-contained without needing the diagram to ask 'what mechanism releases the tricing pendant,' so it's usable as a definition-style question, but the diagram itself cannot be reliably reproduced/labeled without a legend we don't have. Complements SIU item 57 (tricing pendants released after all embarked — timing, not mechanism).

items.originalQuestion: BOTH INTERNATIONAL & INLAND If you saw flames aboard a vessel but could see the vessel was not on fire, what would this indicate?
items.originalAnswer: The vessel required immediate assistance
items.cleanedQuestion: Under the Navigation Rules, if you see flames aboard a vessel that is clearly not actually on fire, what does this indicate?
items.cleanedAnswer: The vessel requires immediate assistance (flames are a recognized distress signal).
items.notesOnAmbiguity: OUT-OF-SCOPE FLAG: same 'BOTH INTERNATIONAL & INLAND' Navigation Rules tag as item 3. This is COLREGS/Inland Rules distress-signal content, not the Water Survival manual's own signaling chapter content (which covers EPIRB/SART/VHF/Morse/lifesaving signal tables specifically). Worth asking Vinci whether Rules of the Road is in scope before using.

items.originalQuestion: Which is the required amount of water for each person in a lifeboat on an oceangoing vessel, on an international voyage?
items.originalAnswer: 3 liters
items.cleanedQuestion: On an international voyage, how much water is required per person aboard a lifeboat on an oceangoing vessel?
items.cleanedAnswer: 3 liters per person.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: IMPORTANT DISTINCTION, not a conflict: this is the total REQUIRED STOWED water supply per person (a regulatory provisioning figure), separate from SIU item 74's '1 pint per day after the first 24 hours' (a daily RATIONING RATE once adrift) and SIU item 60's 'one third the daily ration, three times daily' (a rationing PACE). All three can be true simultaneously and would make an excellent compare/contrast cluster once verified, but should not be merged into one fact.

items.originalQuestion: As a vessel sinks to a depth of 15 feet, the hydrostatic trip releases the liferaft container from its cradle by _________.
items.originalAnswer: releasing the tie-down strap
items.cleanedQuestion: As a sinking vessel reaches a depth of 15 feet, how does the hydrostatic trip release the liferaft container from its cradle?
items.cleanedAnswer: By releasing the tie-down strap holding the container to the cradle.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Specific regulatory depth (15 feet) — a precise, testable fact.

items.originalQuestion: What is the function of the hand holds or straps on the underside of an inflatable liferaft?
items.originalAnswer: To right the raft if it capsizes
items.cleanedQuestion: What is the function of the hand holds or straps on the underside of an inflatable liferaft?
items.cleanedAnswer: To right the raft if it capsizes.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Complements the live app's existing (non-SIU) question about using the CO2-bottle side and righting strap — this is the handhold/strap piece of the same overall righting procedure.

items.originalQuestion: BOTH INTERNATIONAL & INLAND Which of the following is TRUE of a distress signal?
items.originalAnswer: May be used separately or with other distress signals
items.cleanedQuestion: Under the Navigation Rules, which statement about distress signals is TRUE?
items.cleanedAnswer: A distress signal may be used separately or in combination with other distress signals.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: OUT-OF-SCOPE FLAG: third and last 'BOTH INTERNATIONAL & INLAND' Navigation Rules item (see items 3, 13). Same scope caution applies.

items.originalQuestion: The steering oar in a lifeboat is usually referred to as the _________.
items.originalAnswer: sweep oar
items.cleanedQuestion: What is the steering oar in a lifeboat usually called?
items.cleanedAnswer: The sweep oar.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Complements SIU item 56 (the steering oar is longer than the others and lashed to the stern) — this gives its NAME, SIU-56 gives its physical description. Together they'd make a strong combined glossary/recall question.

items.originalQuestion: What is the approximate length of the painter of the inflatable liferaft?
items.originalAnswer: 100 feet
items.cleanedQuestion: What is the approximate length of an inflatable liferaft's painter?
items.cleanedAnswer: About 100 feet.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: NOT a real overlap with SIU item 52 despite the shared keyword 'painter' — SIU-52 is a lifeboat sea painter length CALCULATION (40ft stowed height / 200ft from bow -> 80ft), a completely different line on a completely different craft. Keep these clearly distinct in the app; do not merge.

items.originalQuestion: What is the purpose of the hydrostatic release on an inflatable liferaft?
items.originalAnswer: To release the raft from the cradle automatically as the ship sinks
items.cleanedQuestion: What is the purpose of the hydrostatic release on an inflatable liferaft?
items.cleanedAnswer: To automatically release the raft from its cradle as the ship sinks — a backup for when no one can launch it manually.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: None significant.

items.originalQuestion: What would be the result of physical exertion on the part of a person who has fallen into cold water?
items.originalAnswer: It will increase the rate of heat loss from the body
items.cleanedQuestion: What effect does physical exertion have on a person who has fallen into cold water?
items.cleanedAnswer: It increases the rate of heat loss from the body.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Officially confirms the 'minimize movement in cold water' principle already used in the app's original (non-SIU) survival-at-sea content — a good independent corroboration.

items.originalQuestion: On a lifeboat equipped with Rottmer-type releasing gear, what does turning the releasing lever release?
items.originalAnswer: both falls at the same time even if the boat has not reached the water
items.cleanedQuestion: On a lifeboat with Rottmer-type releasing gear, what happens when the releasing lever is turned?
items.cleanedAnswer: Both falls release at the same time — even if the boat has not yet reached the water.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: TOP-PRIORITY MATCH: nearly word-for-word identical to SIU intake item 14, AND this exact fact is already a LIVE app question (q-ch2-rottmer-hard, added in the SIU-inspired enrichment batch). An official USCG sample exam independently confirms this specific, safety-critical fact. This is the single strongest multi-source-agreement case in the entire Q445 intake — recommend promoting q-ch2-rottmer-hard's sourceConfidence immediately.

items.originalQuestion: Puncture leaks in the lower tubes or bottom of an inflatable liferaft should FIRST be stopped by using which item(s)?
items.originalAnswer: Sealing clamps
items.cleanedQuestion: What should be used FIRST to stop a puncture leak in the lower tubes or bottom of an inflatable liferaft?
items.cleanedAnswer: Sealing clamps.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: The 'FIRST' emphasis suggests a sequencing/misconception-trap angle (versus tube patches or repair tape, which sound equally plausible) — good candidate to reclassify as misconception-trap once verified.

items.originalQuestion: You abandoned ship in the North Atlantic and one of the crew members is hypothermic. During a SAR helicopter extraction which lifting device should be avoided when hoisting this patient to the aircraft?
items.originalAnswer: The rescue sling
items.cleanedQuestion: A hypothermic crew member needs SAR helicopter extraction. Which lifting device should be avoided when hoisting this patient?
items.cleanedAnswer: The rescue sling — a hypothermic patient may lack the strength/consciousness to use it safely, unlike a litter, net, or basket.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: High-value: Chapter 6 (Being Rescued) got ZERO items from the SIU intake, so this is genuinely new coverage for an under-served chapter. Strong misconception-trap since a rescue sling is a normal-seeming hoist device that becomes dangerous for a specific patient condition.

items.originalQuestion: Which is TRUE concerning a certified lifeboat operator assigned to command a lifeboat?
items.originalAnswer: Must have a list of the persons assigned to the lifeboat
items.cleanedQuestion: What is true concerning a certified lifeboat operator assigned to command a lifeboat?
items.cleanedAnswer: They must have a list of the persons assigned to that lifeboat.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: NEAR-VERBATIM MATCH to SIU intake item 5. Official confirmation of a fact that has NOT yet been promoted into a live app question — strong candidate to add as a new question with multi-source confidence from the start.

items.originalQuestion: You will find a limit switch on which type of davit?
items.originalAnswer: Gravity davit
items.cleanedQuestion: On which type of davit would you find a limit switch?
items.cleanedAnswer: A gravity davit.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: INTERNAL DUPLICATE within this same Q445 source: item 39 asks the identical question with the same answer, just among different distractor choices. Only one of the two should become an app question.

items.originalQuestion: Where would the number 2 lifeboat on a tanker be located?
items.originalAnswer: The forwardmost boat on the port side
items.cleanedQuestion: On a tanker, where would lifeboat #2 be located?
items.cleanedAnswer: The forwardmost boat on the port side.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Reinforces the same even-numbers-port, forward-to-aft numbering convention as SIU item 11 (lifeboat #2 forward of #4 on port side) — consistent, not contradictory. Together with items 29 and 31 below, this forms a strong 4-item numbering cluster.

items.originalQuestion: A motor lifeboat shall carry sufficient fuel to operate continuously for a period of which duration?
items.originalAnswer: 24 hours
items.cleanedQuestion: A motor lifeboat must carry sufficient fuel to operate continuously for how long?
items.cleanedAnswer: 24 hours.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: TOP-PRIORITY MATCH: this is the exact regulatory figure (24 hours) already live in the app as q-ch2-24hrfuel, which currently carries an explicit 'unverified, needs checking' caveat because it came only from the unverified SIU practice test (item 25, also '24hrs of continuous operation'). An official USCG sample exam now independently confirms the identical figure. This directly resolves that caveat — recommend updating q-ch2-24hrfuel's sourceConfidence and removing/softening its unverified warning.

items.originalQuestion: Your vessel has 3 lifeboats on each side. The middle lifeboat on the port side is designated as which boat number?
items.cleanedQuestion: A vessel has 3 lifeboats on each side. What number is the middle lifeboat on the port side?
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Part of the numbering cluster with items 27 and 31 and SIU item 11 — together these fully specify the even/port, odd/starboard, forward-to-aft numbering convention across multiple boats per side.

items.originalQuestion: When using a handheld smoke signal in a liferaft, at which location should you activate the signal?
items.originalAnswer: On the downwind side of the liferaft
items.cleanedQuestion: When using a handheld smoke signal in a liferaft, where should you activate it?
items.cleanedAnswer: On the downwind side of the liferaft.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: INTERNAL NEAR-DUPLICATE within this Q445 source: item 34 asks the same fact for 'survival craft' generally rather than 'liferaft' specifically, with the same answer (downwind side). Consider keeping only one as an app question, or explicitly note they generalize the same rule.

items.originalQuestion: Your vessel has 3 lifeboats on each side. What is the designated boat number of the aftermost boat on the starboard side?
items.cleanedQuestion: A vessel has 3 lifeboats on each side. What number is the aftermost boat on the starboard side?
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Completes the numbering cluster with items 27, 29, and SIU item 11.

items.originalQuestion: What is the name given to the lines that are passed around the falls to hold the boat and prevent it from swinging violently?
items.originalAnswer: Frapping lines
items.cleanedQuestion: What are the lines called that are passed around the falls to hold a lifeboat and prevent it from swinging violently while being lowered?
items.cleanedAnswer: Frapping lines.
items.mappedSubtopic: Launching a Lifeboat from a Gravity Davit
items.notesOnAmbiguity: This is arguably a BETTER, more complete definition of frapping lines than the one currently live in q-gloss-frapping (added from SIU item 16). Recommend using this official phrasing to strengthen that question's explanation. Also complements item 11 above (same fact, restated).

items.originalQuestion: Which statement is TRUE concerning distress signals in a survival craft?
items.originalAnswer: Handheld flares and orange smoke signals are required.
items.cleanedQuestion: Which statement about required distress signals in a survival craft is TRUE?
items.cleanedAnswer: Handheld flares and orange smoke signals are required equipment.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Specific equipment-requirement fact; worth double-checking exact required quantities before treating as final.

items.originalQuestion: When using a handheld smoke signal aboard a survival craft, how should you activate the signal?
items.originalAnswer: On the downwind side of the craft
items.cleanedQuestion: When using a handheld smoke signal aboard a survival craft, how should you activate it?
items.cleanedAnswer: On the downwind side of the craft.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: INTERNAL NEAR-DUPLICATE of item 30 (same rule, 'liferaft' vs. general 'survival craft' framing). Marked false here in favor of item 30 to avoid a redundant pair; keep whichever framing better matches how Vinci's course generally refers to survival craft.

items.originalQuestion: What is the function of the lifeboat davit limit switches?
items.originalAnswer: To cut off power to the winch when the lifeboat nears the final stowed position
items.cleanedQuestion: What is the function of lifeboat davit limit switches?
items.cleanedAnswer: To cut off power to the winch when the lifeboat nears its final stowed position.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Complements items 26/39 (which davit type has limit switches) by explaining what the switches actually do — good three-part cluster (davit type + function).

items.originalQuestion: After having thrown the liferaft and stowage container into the water, how is the liferaft inflated?
items.originalAnswer: Pulling on the painter line
items.cleanedQuestion: After throwing the liferaft and its stowage container into the water, how is the liferaft inflated?
items.cleanedAnswer: By pulling on the painter line.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: None significant.

items.originalQuestion: Provided every effort is used to produce, as well as preserve body moisture content by avoiding perspiration, how long is it normally possible to survive without stored quantities of water?
items.originalAnswer: 8 to 14 days
items.cleanedQuestion: If every effort is made to produce and preserve body moisture (avoiding perspiration), how long can a person normally survive without stored water?
items.cleanedAnswer: 8 to 14 days.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: New official figure with no direct SIU counterpart — SIU covered rationing pace/amount but not overall survival duration without water.

items.originalQuestion: What is the primary advantage of using a sea anchor with the survival craft?
items.originalAnswer: It reduces your drift rate
items.cleanedQuestion: What is the PRIMARY advantage of using a sea anchor with a survival craft?
items.cleanedAnswer: It reduces your drift rate — this source specifically calls this the primary advantage.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Near-verbatim match to SIU item 29 ('reduces the drift rate'). Useful ranking detail: this source explicitly calls drift reduction the PRIMARY advantage, which helps resolve any ambiguity in a compare/contrast question against broaching-prevention (SIU item 55) as a secondary/different benefit of the same equipment.

items.originalQuestion: Limit switches are used on which davits?
items.originalAnswer: Gravity davits
items.cleanedQuestion: Limit switches are used on which type of davit?
items.cleanedAnswer: Gravity davits.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: INTERNAL DUPLICATE of item 26 (identical question and answer, different distractor set). Marked false here in favor of item 26.

items.originalQuestion: Which action should be taken once fueling has been completed?
items.originalAnswer: All hatches should be opened and all compartments should be ventilated
items.cleanedQuestion: Once fueling is completed, what action should be taken?
items.cleanedAnswer: All hatches should be opened and all compartments should be ventilated.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Companion step to item 10 (ventilate before starting the engine) — these are sequential parts of the same post-fueling safety procedure, not conflicting facts. Consider combining into one multi-step procedure question.

items.originalQuestion: You have abandoned ship in tropical waters. Which procedure(s) should be used during a prolonged period in a raft?
items.originalAnswer: All of the above (get plenty of rest / keep entrance curtains open / wet clothes during the day to decrease perspiration)
items.cleanedQuestion: After abandoning ship in tropical waters, which procedures should be followed during a prolonged period in a raft?
items.cleanedAnswer: Get plenty of rest, keep entrance curtains open, and wet clothes during the day to decrease perspiration — all three.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: High value: the live app's survival content is almost entirely cold-water-focused; this is new coverage for the hot/tropical-environment side of the same subtopic, with no SIU counterpart.

items.originalQuestion: Which is the most common type of davit found on ocean service merchant vessels today?
items.originalAnswer: Gravity
items.cleanedQuestion: What is the most common type of davit found on ocean service merchant vessels today?
items.cleanedAnswer: Gravity davits.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: None significant.

items.originalQuestion: Which statement concerning satellite EPIRBs is TRUE?
items.originalAnswer: Once activated, these EPIRBs continuously send up a signal for use in identifying the vessel and for determining the position of the beacon.
items.cleanedQuestion: Which statement about satellite EPIRBs is TRUE?
items.cleanedAnswer: Once activated, a satellite EPIRB continuously transmits a signal used to identify the vessel and determine the beacon's position.
items.mappedSubtopic: Emergency Position Indicating Radio Beacon / EPIRB
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Related equipment to SIU item 75 (turn SART/EPIRB ON before abandoning) but a different fact (ongoing transmission behavior vs. pre-abandonment action) — complementary, not overlapping.

items.originalQuestion: When transferring survivors from an enclosed lifeboat to a rescue vessel, personnel on board the boat should take which action?
items.originalAnswer: Remain seated inside and make the transfer one person at a time
items.cleanedQuestion: When transferring survivors from an enclosed lifeboat to a rescue vessel, what should personnel aboard the boat do?
items.cleanedAnswer: Remain seated inside and transfer one person at a time.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Directly confirms the live app's existing (non-SIU) content about sequential, crew-directed transfer during rescue — official corroboration of that same reasoning pattern, now specifically for lifeboat-to-vessel transfer. Also fills in Chapter 6, which got zero SIU coverage.

items.originalQuestion: If an inflatable liferaft is overturned, it may be righted by which action?
items.originalAnswer: Standing on the inflating cylinder and pulling on the righting straps
items.cleanedQuestion: If an inflatable liferaft is overturned, how may it be righted?
items.cleanedAnswer: Stand on the inflating (CO2) cylinder and pull on the righting straps.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Confirms the live app's existing (non-SIU) question about righting via the CO2-bottle side — official corroboration of an already-live fact.

items.originalQuestion: What is the purpose of the tricing pendants?
items.originalAnswer: They hold a lifeboat next to the embarkation deck while loading
items.cleanedQuestion: What is the purpose of the tricing pendants?
items.cleanedAnswer: They hold the lifeboat snug against the embarkation deck while it is being loaded.
items.mappedSubtopic: Launching a Lifeboat from a Gravity Davit
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Complements SIU item 57 (tricing pendants released after everyone has embarked) — purpose vs. release timing, same equipment. Also relevant alongside item 12 (mechanism that releases them).

items.originalQuestion: If an inflatable liferaft inflates upside down, you can right it by taking which action?
items.originalAnswer: Standing on the CO2 bottle, holding the bottom straps, and throwing your weight backwards
items.cleanedQuestion: If an inflatable liferaft inflates upside down, how can it be righted?
items.cleanedAnswer: Stand on the CO2 bottle, hold the bottom straps, and throw your weight backward.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: TOP-PRIORITY MATCH: this closely matches the live app's existing (non-SIU) righting-procedure question, right down to the CO2-bottle-side leverage technique. Strong, independent official confirmation of already-live content — recommend using this as the basis to upgrade that question's sourceConfidence.

items.originalQuestion: How should the sea painter of a lifeboat be led?
items.originalAnswer: Forward and outside of all obstructions
items.cleanedQuestion: How should a lifeboat's sea painter be led (routed)?
items.cleanedAnswer: Forward, and outside of all obstructions.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Complements the live app's existing sea-painter questions (q-ch2-seapainteruse, q-gloss-seapainter, both from the SIU-inspired batch) and the SIU intake's sea-painter cluster (items 18, 22, 45, 46, 59, 79) by adding the ROUTING detail (forward, outside obstructions) none of them currently cover.

items.originalQuestion: When launching a lifeboat, when should frapping lines be rigged?
items.originalAnswer: When the boat is at the embarkation deck
items.cleanedQuestion: When launching a lifeboat, when should frapping lines be rigged?
items.cleanedAnswer: When the boat is at the embarkation deck.
items.mappedSubtopic: Launching a Lifeboat from a Gravity Davit
items.notesOnAmbiguity: TOP-PRIORITY MATCH: essentially verbatim agreement with SIU item 16 ('frapping lines should be rigged at the embarkation deck') AND the live app's q-gloss-frapping question already reflects this same timing. Strong multi-source confirmation — recommend upgrading q-gloss-frapping's sourceConfidence using this item plus item 11/32 above for the function.

items.originalQuestion: An emergency sea anchor may be constructed by using which item(s)?
items.originalAnswer: All of the above (an oar and canvas weighted down / an air tank filled with water / a boat bucket)
items.cleanedQuestion: What can be used to construct an emergency (improvised) sea anchor?
items.cleanedAnswer: An oar and canvas weighted down, an air tank filled with water, or a boat bucket — any of these will work.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: New, practically valuable content with no SIU counterpart — improvised equipment construction is a good 'what do you do when the standard equipment fails' scenario.
